title: Thinking in User Stories
intro: User stories describe features from the user's perspective. Judge whether each one is well-written or needs improvement.

teach.title: What Are User Stories?
teach.body: A user story describes a feature from the perspective of the person using it. The format is simple: **As a [user], I want to [action], so that [benefit]**. This forces you to think about WHY a feature exists, not just what it does.

teach.title: User Story Examples
teach.code:
GOOD: "As a user, I want to set a due date
       on my tasks, so that I can prioritize
       what needs to be done first."

BAD:  "Add a date picker component"

GOOD: "As a user, I want to filter tasks by
       status, so that I can focus on what's
       in progress."

BAD:  "Implement filtering"
teach.annotation: The good examples explain WHO wants it, WHAT they want, and WHY. The bad ones are just technical tasks with no context.

teach.body: User stories keep you focused on solving real problems for real users. When you're tempted to add a feature, write it as a user story first. If you can't explain the 'so that' part, the feature might not be needed.

cards.content: As a user, I want to create tasks with a title and optional description, so that I can capture what I need to do
cards.explanation: Clear user, clear action, clear benefit. This is a well-formed user story.

cards.content: Implement a REST API endpoint POST /api/tasks that accepts a JSON body
cards.explanation: This is an implementation detail, not a user story. It describes HOW, not WHAT or WHY.

cards.content: As a user, I want to see only my incomplete tasks, so that I can focus on what still needs to be done
cards.explanation: The 'so that' clause explains why filtering matters — it's about focus, not just UI.

cards.content: Users should be able to do stuff with tasks
cards.explanation: Way too vague. What stuff? 'Do stuff' could mean anything. User stories need specific actions.

cards.content: As a user, I want to mark tasks as complete, so that I can track my progress and feel accomplished
cards.explanation: Notice the emotional benefit — 'feel accomplished'. Understanding user motivation leads to better design.

cards.content: As a user, I want to add tags to tasks
cards.explanation: Missing the 'so that' part. Without a reason, this might be scope creep. Why do they need tags? To organize? To search? The reason matters.
